cards.back: When a derived class object is assigned to a base class object, the derived-specific parts are 'sliced off', leaving only the base class portion

cards.question: What happens in this code?
cards.answer: Object slicing occurs. Only the Base part of d is copied to b. derived_val is lost.
cards.explanation: When assigning a Derived object to a Base object, the compiler copies only the Base portion. The derived-specific members are sliced off.

cards.text: Object slicing occurs when passing a derived object {{c1::by value}} to a function that takes a base class parameter.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Object slicing occurs when d is passed by value. The Base copy constructor creates a Base object, losing the Derived parts. Virtual functions don't help because b is a Base object, not a reference or pointer.

cards.front: How can you prevent object slicing?
cards.back:
• Pass by reference or pointer instead of by value
  • Delete the copy constructor and assignment operator in base classes
  • Use std::unique_ptr or std::shared_ptr for polymorphic objects

cards.text: To prevent object slicing in polymorphic base classes, pass parameters by {{c1::reference}} or {{c2::pointer}}, not by value.

cards.question: How would you fix this function to prevent slicing?
cards.answer: Change to: void processAnimal(const Animal& a) or void processAnimal(Animal& a)
cards.explanation: Using a reference parameter prevents slicing. The reference refers to the original object, preserving its type and enabling virtual function resolution.

To prevent object slicing at compile time, delete the copy constructor and copy assignment operator in polymorphic base classes:

This prevents derived objects from being copied as base objects, forcing users to use references or pointers.

cards.front: What happens if you store derived objects in a std::vector<Base>?
cards.back:
Object slicing occurs. Each derived object is sliced to just its Base part when inserted into the vector. Virtual functions won't work polymorphically.

Use std::vector<Base*> or std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Base>> instead.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Object slicing! Animals only contains Animal objects. Cat and Dog are sliced when inserted. All speak() calls return Animal::speak().
cards.explanation: Store polymorphic objects by pointer: std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Animal>> animals; animals.push_back(std::make_unique<Cat>("Felix"));

Polymorphism requires:
  • The actual object type to be preserved
  • Calling through a pointer or reference
Object slicing violates both:
  • Creates a new object of the base type
  • Loses all derived-class information
  • Virtual functions resolve to base versions because it IS a base object now
examples: Base b = d;        // Slicing - b is a Base object
Base& ref = d;    // No slicing - ref refers to Derived
